With limited information, you can tell this is a cross-run triage surface, not a full diagnosis. It clearly separates call-review coverage, segment-review coverage, and the shape of negative signals.
The obvious part is that call coverage is complete in the shown slice while segment coverage is not. It is also obvious that `tokio-rs__tokio-5200` is one of the most degraded runs in the table.

The non-obvious part is that the segment rows are mostly not saying "bad review"; they are saying "anchor mismatch". That means the main issue is version alignment between segment reviews and the latest segmentation basis.

The dataset looks heavily skewed toward a small number of runs with concentrated issue load. It also looks like call-level review is broadly usable, while segment-level review quality is limited by persistence/anchor compatibility.

`Calls` and `Segments` are coverage ratios: reviewed items over total items for the selected run. `Issues` is the count of call-review rows that the aggregate classifies as non-focused or otherwise problematic, `Gaps` is missing call reviews plus missing segment reviews, and `Skips` is segment reviews rejected because they do not match the selected anchor basis.

From the commands you can derive coverage, top issue density, mismatch rate, and whether a run is mostly stable or structurally degraded. You can also identify which tool calls are most associated with search thrash and which segments lack a usable review.

The graphs show distribution shape, not a new metric. `Coverage shape` is the per-segment coverage sparkline, `Issue severity` is the per-call-issue severity sparkline, and the histogram section buckets those same values so you can see whether pain is concentrated or spread out.

A reasonable assumption is that high issue counts plus low segment usability usually mean the run is a poor candidate for trusting segment-level interpretation. Another reasonable assumption is that `anchor mismatch` reflects re-segmentation drift rather than random failure.

The biggest missing unlock is a breakdown of issues by category, not just total count. Next would be explicit anchor lineage, clearer separation of segment-review absence versus mismatch, per-turn ordering, and a cross-run ranking by dominant failure mode.

The least meaningful piece right now is the segment coverage histogram, because it is effectively binary under the current implementation. It is readable, but it compresses too much into `0` versus `1` to explain much beyond whether a review exists for the current anchor.

If I were designing the system, I would first fix anchor stability or make lineage explicit so segment reviews remain reusable across re-segmentation. Then I would add a per-run failure taxonomy so the aggregate can rank intervention targets instead of only reporting density.
I would change the label `Signal histograms` because it sounds more semantic than it is. A more literal label like `Coverage + Severity Distribution` would better match what the data actually shows.

I would keep the compact all-runs summary with the two progress bars. It is a good overview primitive because it gives an immediate sense of call completeness versus segment usability without forcing a deep dive.
